steps.text: Inspect general areas of the facility for combustible dust hazards.
Inspect dust-associated equipment in the facility.
Inspect hazardous (classified) locations requiring safe electrical equipment.
Prepare a preliminary inspection plan.
Ask appropriate questions and request up-to-date documents during the inspection.
Document observations.
Take photos and measurements.
Request, collect and study provided documents.
Draw pictures and write notes.
Conduct interviews.
Evaluate the evidence collected.
Perform evaluations of electrical equipment in potentially hazardous locations.
Request information from the employer regarding electrically classified locations.
Inspect the classified locations in the facility.
Ensure electrical equipment in classified locations is intrinsically safe, approved, or safe for the locations.
Assess that powered industrial trucks used in hazardous locations are appropriate.
Review Appendix C, Common Inspection Questions section of this chapter.
Confirm information about the hazards and characteristics of the combustible dust(s).
Ask the employer for drawings and other applicable documents to assist in walk-arounds.
Discuss the on-site combustible dust generating processes and associated equipment with the employer.
Discuss PPE and area entry requirements with the employer.
Wear the proper PPE into the field.
Document conditions in the field during the initial walk-around.
Take dust accumulation measurements.
Review applicable OSHA standard requirements and/or industry recognition of hazards and methods of abatement.
Communicate any documented hazards to the employer.
Ask managers and employees if they are aware of the hazards of the combustible dust(s) at your facility.
Ask managers for a copy of the identified dust hazards.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the training records.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the incident reports.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the OSHA 300 forms and/or other reports.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the procedures and work practices.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the procedure(s) for housekeeping.

preconditions.text: When CSHOs suspect that the dusts to be sampled may have MIEs at or below 30 mJ, CSHOs should either collect the samples as low-MIE dusts or contact their supervisor for direction.

constraints.text: PPE must include a hard hat, safety glasses, and FRC. Respirators may also be required, but only if the CSHO is medically approved and fit-tested for respirator use.
Additional PPE may be necessary when low-MIE combustible dusts are encountered.
Fire and/or explosion potential ignition sources from upstream operations generating heat, sparks, embers, or fire.
Electrostatic build-up/discharge via any isolated conductors.
Filter failure and subsequent introduction of particulate into fan.
Sparking from introduction of foreign materials such as tramp metal or stones.
Potential dust cloud formation during filter cleaning.
Venting indoors with standard vent panels is considered hazardous.
Venting systems need to be designed, installed, and maintained using the manufacturer’s recommendations.
